EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This flooding occurred in a large portion of northeast Montana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Musselshell River at Mosby reached close to 16 feet on May 23rd (the second highest stage ever), 8 feet above flood stage. Most of these creeks remained high or out of their banks until the end of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were five fires in the Lincoln Ranger District. All five of these wildfires were sparked by lightning that moved through the Lincoln area Saturday evening, October 1, 2011. The Arrastra, Lone Point and Porcupine fires are burning in the Arrastra Creek area; the Baking Powder fire is burning in the Scapegoat Wilderness between Baking Powder Creek and Falls Creek, and the Dalton Mountain fire. Combined the fires burned about 734 acres in steep terrain. Cool temperatures, higher relative humidity levels and precipitation that occurred in Lincoln following ignition helped minimize activity and spread of the Lincoln fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were five fires in the Lincoln Ranger District. All five of these wildfires were sparked by lightning that moved through the Lincoln area Saturday evening, October 1, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, fast moving Pacific storm moved a cross western Montana as November came to a close. The heaviest snowfall was orographically influenced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Lolo Pass measured 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Considerable forcing and ascent and deep-layer wind fields resulted in widespread and intense thunderstorm activity. Boundary layer dewpoints in the 50s, steep lapse rates and pronounced veering of winds with height fostered an environment favorable for supercells capable of large hail and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storage shed was blown apart and flew 60-70 feet into a center pivot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Carbon County officials reported that a rancher was struck and killed by lightning in the Pryor Mountains. The Sheriff's department said a 54-year-old man was rounding up cattle for branding at a ranch about 12 miles east of Bridger when he and his horse were struck about 1:30 pm on June 11th. The man and his horse died at the scene.

A mid-level impulse moved across the Northern Rockies. Large-scale forcing combined with low-level upslope flow resulted in thunderstorm development across portions of southeast and south central Montana. Steep Lapse rates, dewpoints in the 50s and 60s, and 40-50kt westerly shear favored the development of strong to severe thunderstorms.

A potent upper trough and unstable airmass brought an increase in severe thunderstorms. Deep shear supported supercells with large hail. Vad wind profiles were indicative of strong directional shear supportive of intense low level updraft rotation resulting in a tornado threat. However...no tornado warnings were issued and no tornadoes were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 70 mph was reported at mile marker 157 on I-94.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong flow of Pacific moisture impacted the west facing slopes of the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains. This resulted in a period of heavy snow to end out the year, and in fact, was the only significant snow of the month. In addition, strong winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers ascended a steep, narrow drainage above Fisher Creek on Henderson Mountain outside of Cooke City. The drainage delivered the riders into steep, open terrain on the northeast face. The victim climbed to approximately 9,800 feet where he triggered the slide. He was able to turn downhill, but was caught as he descended the slope. His head was buried 2-3 feet deep and the heel of his boot was sticking out of the snow. He was found and uncovered in approximately 10-12 minutes, but did not survive. The crown of the avalanche ranged from 1-4 feet deep and 300-400 feet wide. The slide ran a total of 1,000 vertical feet. The slope angle at the trigger point was 35 degrees, but increased to 40 degrees near the upper portion of the crown. The alpha angle was 33 degrees. The debris ranged from 7-10 feet deep near the toe of the slide. More detailed information can be found at www.mtavalanche.com.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across southern Park County during the late afternoon hours of Tuesday July 26. A woman leading a horseback ride in a wooded area at about 530pm in the Chico area was struck by lightning. A man performed CPR on the victim before she was flown to Livingston Memorial Hospital then to Billings Clinic. Another woman was treated at the scene for burns while a 6-year-old girl was treated for minor injuries at the hospital. Two horses were killed in the incident. Lightning strikes were fairly isolated in the region at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cattle struck by lightning standing near a fence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty one heifers were killed when lightning struck south-southwest of Wibaux.  The heifers were generally lined up along a fence line, but there were six of them that were scattered up to 75 yards away.  Some of the fencing was also knocked down.  There were a total of 51 heifers in the pasture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture moving over a cold airmass east of the Continetal Divide produced areas of heavy snow. Snow amounts greater than 10 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft produced high winds along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front. Sustained winds in excess of 40 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds of 42 mph were reported for more than an hour at the Cut Bank ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front accompanied by a line of showers and thunderstorms moved through Billings during the early morning hours of the 11th. The thunderstorms produced a microburst with winds estimated at 80 mph around 1AM MST in the Billings Heights causing a narrow corridor of damage. ||A damage assessment team from the National Weather Service in Billings traveled to El Rancho Drive in the High Sierra Subdivision in the Billings Heights. They found numerous vinyl fences blown down, some roof shingle and siding damage, bent street signs, tossed around trampolines and downed free-standing basketball hoops. ||The damage pattern, which showed objects blown down from Northwest to Southeast, was consistent with severe thunderstorm straight-line winds. The damage was determined to be caused by a microburst with winds around 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst resulted in damage to numerous vinyl fences, some roof shingles and house siding along El Rancho Drive in Billings Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An very unstable environment and deep-layer shear combined with plentiful surface moisture to produce severe thunderstorms and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported quarter-sized hail stones along with 40 to 50 mph winds and measured 0.71 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An very unstable environment and deep-layer shear combined with plentiful surface moisture to produce severe thunderstorms and a few tornadoes.
A strong pressure gradient across the Upper Stillwater River Valley resulted in high winds through the Beartooth Foothills.
Ice jam has forced Gallatin River near Logan out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged period of cold temperatures resulted in an ice jam which forced the river out of its banks near the river gage site. Below freezing temperatures persisted prolonging the occurrence of breakup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1244 MST, King Coulee RAWS reported a measured wind gust of 58 mph, At 1506 MST, Nashua reported sustained winds over 45 mph with sustained winds over 40 mph since 1155 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressively moist weather system and associated cold front moved across north central Idaho and western Montana. Intense lift due to the passing cold front and an upper level jet produced areas of heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 3 inches of snow in 2 hours and Lost trail pass received 3 inches of snow in 2 hours. Whiteout conditions were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern was over the northern Rockies with a disturbance |moving across southern Idaho. A well organized mid level circulation produced some significant snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bart Bonney, the DES coordinator reported 7 inch snowfall at Georgetown Lake and 10 inches north of highway 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern was over the northern Rockies with a disturbance |moving across southern Idaho. A well organized mid level circulation produced some significant snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anaconda spotter at 5800 feet reported 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hillside DOT sensor had sustained winds of over 40 mph with peak gusts near 60 mph. The Jordan ASOS had sustained winds over 40 mph with frequent gusts over 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plentywood reported sustained winds between 35 and 50 mph with a gust to 61 mph while the Crosby, ND RAWS reported a gust to 78 mph. Whiteout conditions were reported by Law Enforcement on Highway 5. Electricity was lost in Wesby with winds estimated to peak at 80 mph. This created drifts to 6 feet deep, uprooted a 300 foot tall pine tree (with a 15 to 20 inch diameter trunk) and rotated a stop sign.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern was over the northern Rockies with a disturbance |moving across southern Idaho. A well organized mid level circulation produced some significant snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts reported were, 11 inches at Warm Springs, elevation 7798 feet, 8 inches at Peterson Meadows, elevation 7200 feet and North Fork Elk Creek, elevation 6250.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jetstream with wind speeds of over 120 knots was over western Montana and north central Idaho. During the day, some of the stronger winds aloft mixed down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Logan Pass visitors center in Glacier National Park at an elevation of 6775 feet, reported west winds of 51 mph with gusts to 94 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing Pacific system moving northeast from California brought snow and blowing snow to areas north of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0700 MST March 23rd, a Coop observer reported 3 inches of new snow. At 1059 MST, Weather Spotter at Medicine Lake reported 5 inches of new snow. This combined with 20 to 30 mph winds resulted in winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing Pacific system moving northeast from California brought snow and blowing snow to areas north of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions ceased at about 2017 MST on March 22nd, but due to the wind, winter storm conditions continued into March 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowmelt and ice jamming contributed to flooding in Garfield county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1510 MST, a private dam failed and was causing flash flooding downstream on the Big Dry Creek. The high water was moving towards an ice jam and towards the town of Jordan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: People's Creek and Beaver Creek swelled due to excessive snowmelt and some ice jamming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tribal Law Enforcement reported Peoples Creek was flooding along the Lodgepole Highway. Flooding also began at Dodson on March 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek flowed out of it's banks due to excessive snowmelt and ice jamming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 23rd, Beaver Creek was out of its banks and the Gauge at Guston Coulee on Beaver Creek was 12.28 feet and continuing to rise. On March 25th, the gauge at Guston Coulee at Beaver Creek spreading out of the creek channel and across the fields and ditches and over a few roads in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent east winds created continuous drifting of snow across portions of the area during the period. Residents in many areas were not able to travel as road crews were unable to keep up with the drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistant easterly winds and a deep loose snowpack contributed to hazardous road conditions. Road departments suspended plowing operations at times as they could not keep up with the drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent east winds created continuous drifting of snow across portions of the area during the period. Residents in many areas were not able to travel as road crews were unable to keep up with the drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistant easterly winds and a deep loose snowpack contributed to hazardous road conditions. Road departments suspended plowing operations at times as they could not keep up with the drifting. The Opheim school was hard to remain open during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm air advection pattern developed ahead of an arctic cold front.   The airmass was rich with pacific moisture and very unstable.  A strong frontogenesis band set up over portions of the Billings Forecast Area which resulted in bands of heavy snow. In addition, gusty northeast winds produced blowing and drifting snow as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hardin and Busby areas received 6-7 inches of snow. The Department of Transportation reported severe driving conditions north of Hardin to Custer due to blowing snow and poor visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jams and snowmelt contributed to the flooding in Petroleum County and along the Musselshell River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding began at 0753 MST on March 13th, with an ice jam breaking a utility pole on the Musselshell before the CMR boundary. Houses in the area were surrounded by water. Also water was running over over the road Trail 79. On March 14th, ice jam causing north of the Mosby bridge causing river to flow out of its banks flooding in all pastures and hay fields. Also spotter 3 miles southeast of Mosby reported flooding with Calf Creek and cannot get back to their homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Spring storm system brought heavy wet snow to much of South Central Montana. The I-90 corridor from Big Timber to Columbus was hit the hardest with up to 2 feet of snow. Brief power outages and road closures were reported in the Reedpoint and Big Timber vicinities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pryor and surrounding areas received between 6 and 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved south out of British Columbia and across Idaho into Wyoming.  Heavy, wet snow occurred across the upslope areas of the Beartooth and Crazy Mountains.  In addition, isentropic flow combined with Pacific moisture ahead of the system hit the eastern zones with 6-8 inches of snow across Custer and Fallon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 inches with 3 foot drifts along with severe driving conditions was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Spring storm system brought heavy wet snow to much of South Central Montana. The I-90 corridor from Big Timber to Columbus was hit the hardest with up to 2 feet of snow. Brief power outages and road closures were reported in the Reedpoint and Big Timber vicinities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Big Timber received 10 inches of snow with up to 36 inches reported across the Eastern Foothills of the Crazy Mountains. In addition, 3 diameter tree branches were broken around the Big Timber area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm brought heavy snow to portions of southwest Montana. The heaviest snow generally fell at elevations above 6000 feet. Snow amounts ranged from 6 inches to nearly two feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell at Garneill 12ENE (the Crystal Lake SNOTEL site - elevation 6050 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly flow aloft with embedded surface lows moving across northern Wyoming brought heavy snow to mainly the southeast corner of Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the county received between 8 and 18 inches of snow along with 28 inch drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly flow aloft with embedded surface lows moving across northern Wyoming brought heavy snow to mainly the southeast corner of Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 12 was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly flow aloft with embedded surface lows moving across northern Wyoming brought heavy snow to mainly the southeast corner of Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ashland received 6 of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm brought heavy snow to portions of southwest Montana. The heaviest snow generally fell at elevations above 6000 feet. Snow amounts ranged from 6 inches to nearly two feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Ulm 12SSE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread snow to North Central Montana. Snow amounts up to 14 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Babb 11WSW (the Many Glacier SNOTEL site - elevation 4900 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread snow to North Central Montana. Snow amounts up to 14 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell at Marysville 2SW (the Great Divide Ski Area).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread snow to North Central Montana. Snow amounts up to 14 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen inches of snow fell at Bynum 24W (the Mount Lockhart SNOTEL site - elevation 6400 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread snow to North Central Montana. Snow amounts up to 14 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven inches of snow fell at Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread snow to North Central Montana. Snow amounts up to 14 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Neihart 21W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread snow to North Central Montana. Snow amounts up to 14 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen inches of snow fell at Belgrade 13ENE (the Sacajawea SNOTEL site - elevation 6550 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McDonald's DOT sensor had sustained winds from 45 to 65 mph. The Wolf Point ASOS only reported winds between 35 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Hill RAWS reported winds sustained above 40 mph with a gust to 64 mph. Blizzard conditions were reported by the Emergency Manager with trucks sliding off of I-94 and stuck in ditches on Highway 7 along with plows. 4 to 5 foot drifts were common. 12 inches of new snow were reported near Carlyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at Fallon were sustained over 35 mph with a peak gust of 67 mph. A gust to 61 mph were reported at Big Sheep RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam helped swell the Big Muddy River during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1312 MST on April 3rd, an ice jam on the Big Muddy backed water up onto the Rock Springs Road in Reserve while the river gauge near Antelope read 14 feet. On April 5th, flooding continued in Reserve and had expanded down into Medicine Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a wet heavy snow to areas near the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 5 inches of wet heavy snow fell that was accompanied by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak winter storm brought light amounts of snow to the area except for south central Valley county, where 6 to 7 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers around Hinsdale (and one 20 miles north of Saco) observed 6 to 7 inches of snow total from this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glendive AWOS reported several hours of sustained winds above 40 mph through 1600 MST with a gust to 67 mph. Lindsay Divide had similar winds with a gust to 59 mph. A small amount of snow fell too with the most being reported (2) at 2 miles west of Bloomfield. Blizzard conditions were reported over most of the county. Power was lost at 0235 MST in the Lindsay area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained at 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 60 mph for several hours. A peak gust of 75 mph was reported at the Sioux Pass DOT station. Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 8 inches, which contributed to the blizzard conditions. Power lines and trees were knocked down by the heavy/wet snow and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved out of the great basin and through Wyoming and brought significant  moisture into the area.  A deep and moist upslope flow produced heavy wet snow over area mountains...mainly on east and north facing slopes.  In addition, very slushy and slick road conditions were reported in the higher foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Fork Shields snotel received 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved out of the great basin and through Wyoming and brought significant  moisture into the area.  A deep and moist upslope flow produced heavy wet snow over area mountains...mainly on east and north facing slopes.  In addition, very slushy and slick road conditions were reported in the higher foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Placer Basin snotel received 14 inches of snow while Cole Creek snotel received 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved out of the great basin and through Wyoming and brought significant  moisture into the area.  A deep and moist upslope flow produced heavy wet snow over area mountains...mainly on east and north facing slopes.  In addition, very slushy and slick road conditions were reported in the higher foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poplar RAWS reported sustained winds of 40 mph while the DOT sensor at the state line reported a gust to 75 mph before power was lost at 0430 MST April 30th. Visibility of only 10 feet and drifts of 3 to 4 feet helped cause vehicles from Bainville to the North Dakota state line get stuck or slide off of U.S. Highway 2. County Emergency Manager reported that the wind and snow combined knocked out power to an estimated 1500 customers in Eastern Roosevelt and Sheridan Counties. Also on April 30th, a box car from a freight train grabbed low hanging power lines and power poles and dragged them along, ripping up fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system located over western North Dakota resulted in very gusty surface winds over the eastern portions of the Billings forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40+ mph were reported near Ekalaka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system located over western North Dakota resulted in very gusty surface winds over the eastern portions of the Billings forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 mph was reported in Baker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall coupled with the melting of record winter snows brought flooding to the area. The flooding continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge at the Bjornberg Bridge rose to above flood stage from April 9th and remained there until April 27th. It crested on the 14th above moderate flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system deepened off of the west coast while an area of high pressure was located east of the divide. This resulted in a strong pressure gradient across western Montana that produced strong winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous downed trees in the Rattlesnake area of Missoula. A tree had been uprooted and fallen on a house, only minor damage reported. |A row of 9 pine trees, 40 feet tall, were uprooted and fell on a car and camper. A 100 year old tree, 60 feet tall fell on a garage damaging the roof and a car next to the garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy wet snow fell over portions of the area, knocking out power and disrupting travel and calving operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the southern portion of the county, spotters reported that the storm began with rain that changed to sleet, then snow. 1.5 inches of new snow fell, and coupled with strong winds, visibilities were down to a quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy wet snow fell over portions of the area, knocking out power and disrupting travel and calving operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven power lines were downed between Winnett and Mosby from the wet/heavy snow. Visibility was also reported to be down to 300 yards. 4 power poles were snapped on Highway 244 due to the heavy snow and 20 to 30 mph winds. 4 inches of snow was reported in the Winnett and Flatwillow areas. At 2044 MST, the Winnett Fire Cheif reported at least 6 to 8 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek continues to flood, mainly due to snowmelt and some ice jamming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USGS river gauge west of Hinsdale reported a reading of 17.41. Spotters reporting the river to be high and running out of it's banks. On April 5th, the Antelope Creek was close to going over the Billingsley Road, west of Glasgow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beaver, Larb and other smaller creeks flooded across Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver and Larb creeks flooded. Saco Flats flooded, surrounding Saco with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall coupled with the melting of record winter snows brought flooding to the area. The flooding continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gauge at Tampico reported the Milk River to be above flood stage, with a crest on April 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beaver, Larb and other smaller creeks flooded across Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek and other tributaries to the Milk River (as well as the Milk River) are flooding. Roads closed included, but not limited to: the North Tampico Road, 6th Avenue, Whately Road, Lenz Road, Canal Road (west of Glasgow), Rahlf Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Beaver, Frenchmen and other creeks flooded across Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many creeks, mainly the Beaver and the Frenchmen continue to flood. Saco Flats flooding continues to surround Saco with flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Beaver, Frenchmen and other creeks flooded across Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River tributaries continued to flood. These tributaries included, but not limited to, Willow, Beaver, Brazil, Antelope and Rock Creeks. Many roads were flooded and closed including the North Tampico Road, 6th Avenue, Whately Road, Lenz Rd, Canal Rd and Rahlf Lane. After April 18th, the northern tributaries were especially flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beaver and Frenchmen creeks were the main flooding tributaries with this episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the tributaries continue. Roads that are closed include the North Tampico Road, 6th Avenue, Whately Rd, Lenz Rd, Canal Rd and Rahlf Rd. On April 24th, many of the creeks receded into their respective banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall coupled with the melting of record winter snows brought flooding to the area. The flooding continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River at the Glasgow gauge rose to flood stage, cresting on April 16th, above Major Flood Stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall coupled with the melting of record winter snows brought flooding to the area. The flooding continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gauge at Nashua rose above flood stage, cresting on the 17th above moderate flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable southwest flow aloft set the stage for thunderstorm development. Some of these storms turned severe, occurring mainly in Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado briefly touched down in open country.  Very brief dust cloud at the surface was observed with no damage.  The funnel cloud and very weak landspout were observed by numerous NWS Glasgow employees.  No wall cloud was present at the time of the event, but one did form after the funnel dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system moved across western Montana producing areas of snow. A strong jet stream brought some high winds ahead of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported tree branches of 3 inch diameter were blown down 9 miles north of Noxon. The wind gust was associated with a narrow snowband.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system moved across western Montana producing areas of snow. A strong jet stream brought some high winds ahead of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula County Airport received 1 inch of snow in 45 minutes with wind gusts of 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system moved across western Montana producing areas of snow. A strong jet stream brought some high winds ahead of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were closed 4 miles south of Missoula due to snow squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system moved across western Montana producing areas of snow. A strong jet stream brought some high winds ahead of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gust of 50 mph blew some shingles off of a house, 10 miles S of Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific storm brought high winds to the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 45 mph for over an hour were reported at Choteau 10NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air was trapped in the valleys of western Montana as a strong Pacific storm system moved over the region. This system had abundent moisture with waves of energy moving across western Montana. The weather associated with this system covered a wide range of types, from freezing rain to heavy snow and a mixture of rain and snow, along with another blast of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park International Airport in Kalispell was closed from 7 am until 12 pm because of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air was trapped in the valleys of western Montana as a strong Pacific storm system moved over the region. This system had abundent moisture with waves of energy moving across western Montana. The weather associated with this system covered a wide range of types, from freezing rain to heavy snow and a mixture of rain and snow, along with another blast of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported 15 miles southeast of Tarkio, the spotter measured 6 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low level jet streak aligned itself over Montana and the atmosphere favored some mixing of those higher wind speeds down to the surface at times.  The associated cold front pushed southward through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph were measured across the county with a peak wind gust of 62 mph measured at the Scobey Airport AWOS sometime between 3:00 AM and 4:00 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in areas of light rain and snow. A Pacific front moved through on the 8th, then Arctic air surged in as an Upper Low moved over Eastern Montana. Snow became heavy at times...mainly across the upslope areas of Southern Big Horn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 6-8 inches of snow were common along the upslope areas near the mountains. The highway patrol also reported slide offs across the area.

The weather associated with this system covered a wide range of types, from freezing rain to heavy snow and a mixture of rain and snow, along with another blast of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist energetic Pacific storm system began to impact the northern Rockies Sunday afternoon and continued into Monday. The heaviest snowfall was across the mountains, but some valley locations picked up significant snowfall as the surface low pressure moved across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported northeast of Essex, with 9 inches measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across Southern Canada combined with high pressure over Idaho and Utah resulted in a tight pressure gradient along the east slopes of the Rocky Mountain Divide. High winds were observed in Stillwater, Sweet Grass and Wheatland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 mph were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed over the area resulting in high winds across Wheatland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kilo RAWS reported a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed over the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. This resulted in strong winds across the area. Locations such as Livingston to Springdale had whiteout conditions due to several inches of fresh snow already on the ground. I-90 from Springdale to Gallatin County was shut down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 60 mph created blowing snow and very poor visibilities. Highway conditions were considered bad near the KOA Campground and Greycliff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed over the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. This resulted in strong winds across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Arctic air mass moved over western Montana.  As the cold air moved south out of Canada, a strong surface pressure gradient produced gusty winds, causing cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill readings of 24 below zero at Deer Lodge, 33 below zero at Helmville, 25 below zero 3 miles north of Avon, 29 below zero at Garrison, 22 below zero at Georgetown Lake and 24 below zero at Homestake Pass were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough of low pressure moved into western Montana from the Pacific. This system had a good moisture feed with a pool of cold air aloft. Heavy snow was the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 1 mile ENE of Elliston in Powell county measured 5.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough of low pressure moved into western Montana from the Pacific. This system had a good moisture feed with a pool of cold air aloft. Heavy snow was the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began the evening of November 4th 3 miles SW of Georgetown Lake in Granite county and continued into the morning of November 5th. There were 8 inches of snow from the start until around noon on the 5th. At 8pm on the 5th the storm total was 10-11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active northwest flow set up over Montana and Idaho, that brought a series of shortwaves into the region over a couple of days. This produced areas of heavy snow, especially in areas that were orographically enhanced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances moved across western Montana over a couple of days. Abundant moisture was associated with these disturbances along with a very strong jet steam. Many locations received heavy snow, especially in the mountains. As the warm and moist air moved into western Montana, pockets of cold air were trapped in some valleys, producing a good freezing rain scenario.  In addition a strong surge of cold arctic air moved across the divide to interact with the warm moist air and produce heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Olney measured 6.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances moved across western Montana over a couple of days. Abundant moisture was associated with these disturbances along with a very strong jet steam. Many locations received heavy snow, especially in the mountains. As the warm and moist air moved into western Montana, pockets of cold air were trapped in some valleys, producing a good freezing rain scenario.  In addition a strong surge of cold arctic air moved across the divide to interact with the warm moist air and produce heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated 9 inches of snow 6 miles SSW of Lolo Hot Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed over the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. This resulted in strong winds across the area. At 1100 MST on January 8th 6.5 inches of new snow at NWS Glasgow. At 1700 MST on January 8th NWS Glasgow received a total of 7.2 inches along with blowing snow. At 1710 MST on January 8th in Nashua heavy snow fell measuring 6 inches.

At 1320 4 miles southwest of Hinsdale 7.5 inches of new snow fell with 2 to 3 foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system deepened off of the west coast while an area of high pressure was located east of the divide. This resulted in a strong pressure gradient across western Montana that produced strong winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down across the Missoula valley, confirmed by the Missoula Sheriff office. Power lines and trees down in the city of Missoula on Orange street, Broadway, Pine and Rattlesnake drive. Rattlesnake drive blocked at Lolo and Greenough streets due to downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across Idaho and Wyoming. As the low moved across Wyoming, pressure gradients became tight across Eastern Montana. The tight pressure gradients  combined with a few inches of snow resulted in blizzard conditions across Fallon and Carter Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around 2 inches along with strong winds resulted in near zero visibilities across the area. Highway 7 was closed between Baker and Ekalaka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Western United States, helping to develop an intense surface low pressure system. This low moved into the northern Plains with intense wrap around cold air, wind and snow. The hardest hit areas were zones close to the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0200 MST, Rockglen Saskatchewan reported several hours with winds sustained over 30 mph and frequent gusts over 45 mph. A Ham Radio operator/weather spotter had sustained winds in the 40s with gusts over 50 mph a few times from 0200 to 1400 MST.

Wind chills reached -30F in some places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0845 MST, a spotter 3 miles southwest of Hoyt reported 12 inches of new snow, some drifts on property were knee deep. At 1010 MST in Glendive, spotter reported 7 inches of new snow and still falling. At 1800 MST, spotter 2 miles west-southwest of Glendive reported 9.8 inches storm total. At 2015 MST in Glendive, cooperative observer reported 10 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snowfall with slushy and snowcovered roadways. Official NWS observation in Glasgow measured 4.7 inches of new snowfall with strong winds from about 2100 MST March 21 to 1700 MST March 22nd. Winds were 30 mph sustained with gusts to 40 mph. 6 miles south of Larslan reported blizzard conditions at 1800 MST March 22nd. 3 miles north of Nashua reported heavy drifting across Highway 438 at 1700 MST March 22nd. Coop observer in Nashua reported 4 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areal flooding in the area and on the Yellowstone River into Richland County due mainly to ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Match 17th, water was flowing out of banks of some of the creeks that fed into the Beaver Creek with water running over Highway 7 12 to 13 miles south of Wibaux near Pine Unit. Highway 7 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some impressive ice jamming on the Yellowstone River brought substantial flooding to Prairie and Dawson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An impressive ice jam on the Yellowstone River contained ice chunks the size of a house according to an observer. This ice jam caused damage to the Shirley pumping plant at the Buffalo Rapids District 2 Irrigation Project in Terry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some impressive ice jamming on the Yellowstone River brought substantial flooding to Prairie and Dawson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 14th, an ice jam on the Interstate 94 bridge near Glendive caused some low land flooding. On March 15th, West Glendive beginning a threat from the Yellowstone. On March 16th, equipment such as tractor and cars had to be moved to higher ground as the Interstate 94 bridge made the ice jamming problem much worse. On March 17th, water was flowing around an ice jam on the north side of Glendive resulting in flooding of lowland close to the river. On March 18th, Dawson County commissioners passed a resolution of emergency declaration due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jams and snowmelt contributed to the flooding in Petroleum County and along the Musselshell River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter located on the Musselshell River near the CMR boundary reported a large ice jam breaking his utility pole and houses in his sub division were surrounded by water. Also reported that water was running over nearby road with water at the base of his house. Several streams like the Calf Creek (3 southeast of Mosby) and South Lodge Creek (15 miles west-northwest of Sand Springs) flowed over roadways deep enough to where residents could not leave their homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areal flooding in the area and on the Yellowstone River into Richland County due mainly to ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 16th, small creeks and streams that were swollen. On March 18th, an ice jam around 9 miles long and extending 5 miles west of Terry downstream to near the I-94 bridge near Fallon was causing flooding on the Yellowstone River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areal flooding in the area and on the Yellowstone River into Richland County due mainly to ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 16th, small creeks and streams that feed the Yellowstone River were swollen. On March 18th, an ice jam near Savage was causing some low land flooding. Also ice jams near Sidney were fluctuating river levels and causing flooding near Richland Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areal flooding in the area and on the Yellowstone River into Richland County due mainly to ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 16th, small creeks and streams that feed the Yellowstone River were swollen. On March 18th, an ice jam near the Dawson County line were causing minor flooding of low lying areas. On the afternoon of the 18th, the ice jam that was causing flooding in the Glendive area, moved out of the area. This ice jam flooding caused an estimated $400 thousand worth of damage to irrigation pumps at the Buffalo Rapids Irrigation pump house. The water level was the highest that the employees had seen since 1972.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areal flooding in the area and on the Yellowstone River into Richland County due mainly to ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 16th, small creeks and streams that feed into the Yellowstone River were swollen. They continued to run high into the 19th causing low land flooding. On the 17th, water along Beaver Creek was high near Highway 7 12 to 13 miles south of Wibaux near Pine Unit, closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowmelt and ice jamming contributed to flooding in Garfield county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 17th, spotters reported the middle fork of the Lodgepole Creek was flooding with water over the roads and closing roads in northwest Garfield county. Also, the Steve Forks Creek and Sand Creeks were running out of their banks causing flooding in adjacent fields. Later in the afternoon of the 17th, Garfield County Sheriff reported a couple of ice jams upstream of Jordan on the Big Dry Creek. This caused a flood threat for the town of Jordan at 0157 on March 18th, flooding parts of the town. Garfield County DES reported that one of the ice jams moved downstream. At 1317 MST March 20th, water levels on the Sand Creek, Little Dry Creek and Big Dry Creek east of Jordan were high due to snowmelt and some cases were running across roads (also with blocked culverts).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: People's Creek and Beaver Creek swelled due to excessive snowmelt and some ice jamming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 20th, the Beaver Creek has risen rapidly and lowland flooding has occurred between Highway 2 and Harb Road. On the 22nd, the Beaver Creek is out of its banks and flooding the fields east of Content. To the north 12 miles southwest of Saco, Beaver Creek is at bankful and at the gauge near Guston Coulee. The river is at 11.58 feet, which is the second highest recorded stage at that location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Redwater River swelled due to above normal snowmelt and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small creeks and streams as well as the Redwater River were out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek flowed out of it's banks due to excessive snowmelt and ice jamming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 24rd, Larb Creek had some minor flooding along it and Beaver Creek at Hinsdale was 11.62 feet which was a rise of 6 feet in two days. On March 30th at Hinsdale on Beaver Creek the gauge rose slightly to over 11.5 feet overnight and Larb Creek in western Valley County has some minor flooding along it. The level on the Beaver Creek at Hinsdale rose slightly to 11.6 feet and Larb Creek is still having minor flooding along it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding in the days before the 13th of February due to snowmelt caused several stream in Southern Petroleum County to rise, and then flow into the Mussellshell River.  The water caused significant ice jamming and lead to the flooding of some houses along the river, and some gravel roads on both the Garfield and Petroleum County side of the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private stock pond (Sage Creek/Jensen Dam) on the Sage Creek partially failed, threatening a couple of county roads before it emptied into the Musselshell River. The roads threatened or flooded were Valentine and Dovetail Roads.  The dam completely failed in April according to a report from Montana DNRC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding in the days before the 13th of February due to snowmelt caused several stream in Southern Petroleum County to rise, and then flow into the Mussellshell River.  The water caused significant ice jamming and lead to the flooding of some houses along the river, and some gravel roads on both the Garfield and Petroleum County side of the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and ice jams contributed to unusually high levels on the Musselshell River. The river flooded homes and closed roads for several weeks. The Musselshell River at Mosby was above flood stage between February 13 and 19 peaking at 13.37 feet on the 15th. It was the highest stage recorded at the site since March 1994.

The water caused significant ice jamming and lead to the flooding of some houses along the river, and some gravel roads on both the Garfield and Petroleum County side of the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and ice jams contributed to unusually high levels on the Musselshell River. The river flooded homes and closed roads for several weeks. The Musselshell River at Mosby was above flood stage between February 13 and 19 peaking at 13.37 feet on the 15th. It was the highest stage recorded at the site since March 1994.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing double-barrel low pressure system over eastern Montana and North Dakota brought blizzard conditions that snarled vehicles in parts of the area. The hardest hit counties were: Richland, Sheridan, Roosevelt and Wibaux. Some roads were closed and the visibilities were down to near zero as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began across Sheridan County at 0600 MST and lasted into the evening.  At 0915 MST, in Wesby snow and blowing snow with winds estimated at 50 to 55 mph creating near whiteout conditions. At 0920 MST, Port of Raymond reported snow  and blowing snow with a visibility of five feet. and sustained winds over 40 mph. Travelers into Saskatchewan are turning around and coming back due to poor driving conditions. At 1156 MST, Crosby ND RAWS had a wind gust of 58 and 60 mph over with the next several hours. Comertown DOT sensor also had wind gusts of 58 to 61 mph lasting through 1500 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing double-barrel low pressure system over eastern Montana and North Dakota brought blizzard conditions that snarled vehicles in parts of the area. The hardest hit counties were: Richland, Sheridan, Roosevelt and Wibaux. Some roads were closed and the visibilities were down to near zero as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0500 MST, blizzard conditions were reported which lasted into the later afternoon. From 0520 MST to 1600, the Wolf Point ASOS and the McDonalds DOT sensors reported sustained winds about 30 mph with gusts up to 50 mph. At 1140 MST, a multiple car pile up happened due to bad driving conditions 10 miles west of Poplar. When Highway Patrol stopped to help people out of the cars, a semi truck crashed into all vehicles in the pile. Up to 16 people were taken to the hospital.

The Sidney AWOS had sustained winds over 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph from 0800 to 1600 MST. Snowing and blowing with strong winds reduced visibilities to less than 1/4 of a mile at 0905 MST. Vehicles were also stopped on Highway 16 due to near zero visibility. At 1128 MST, Highway 201 west of Fairview and Highway 16 from Sidney to Culbertson were closed by the county Sheriffs department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and complex weather system from the Pacific moved across north central Idaho to end the year. This system had 3 major impacts on the region. First, a tight surface pressure gradient, along with strong upper level winds produced high winds. Second, abundant moisture with this system produced some heavy snowfall over the mountains, but since the air mass was warm, the lower elevations received a rain and snow mix or just rain. Third, the warm, moist air mass pruduced areas of heavy rain which produced rock slides and released ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was reported 4 miles west-northwest of Darby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moving out of Wyoming resulted in large-scale forcing across Southeast Montana. Very warm and moist air resulted in MLCAPE values of 1500-2500 J/KG. In addition, veering winds with height with 35-40kt of deep-layer shear supported an environment capable of producing supercell thunderstorms.

A significant spring storm impacted south central Montana May 8-11 as a moist and slow moving storm system impacted the region. Rain began to move into the region late Sunday, May 8th with the heaviest rainfall Monday into Tuesday. Some embedded thunderstorms enhanced rainfall across portions of the region late on Monday, the 9th. Widespread rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches occurred. The heavy rainfall also combined with some mid elevation snowmelt from nearby mountains to enhance flooding in some foothill locations. As a result, widespread lowland flooding was reported along with some damage to roads and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred in the Briarwood Subdivision and golf course in South Billings along Blue Creek. Several homes, structures and barns were inundated with water with evacuations required in the area. Damage occurred to the golf course due to the flooding and several bridges washed out on the course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small EF-1 tornado occurred 8 miles south of Terry, MT.  The NWS Glasgow office did a damage survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 8:27pm on May 20, a tornado struck a ranch 8 miles south of Terry on 10 Mile Road. The tornado was observed by a resident of the house on the ranch. The house itself received no damage, though it was about 150 feet away from observed damage. Significant damage occurred to 4 outbuildings on the ranch. There were no injuries and the horses on the ranch were uninjured as they were out in pasture at the time of the tornado.  The damage path was about 500 feet and the width was 200 feet. Based on the damage, the wind speed was determined to be 90 mph or a low end EF-1 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable southwest flow aloft set the stage for thunderstorm development. Some of these storms turned severe, occurring mainly in Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm/ranch was significantly damaged by a tornado and damaging downburst winds.  The storm stripped trees bare, four car garages were either destroyed or damaged.  A 1000 gallon fuel tank that was filled that day was blown off it's stand.  The houses received significant damage from hail and the high winds,and the majority of the windows on the two homes were busted out by the wind and hail.  A 2x6 piece of lumber was blown and implanted into the side of one of the houses.  The majority of the damage was downstream with the storm system.  The peak gust was recorded at 86 mph before the sensor was destroyed.  Quarter size hail fell with the storm as well, wiping out nearby field.  The NWS did not receive the tornado report until a month after the event, but radar data verified the possibility of a tornado, and the property owner verified seeing a funnel cloud come from the sky prior to seeking shelter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 70 mph was reported 20 miles NW of Alzada. Tree tops were torn off and the spotter could feel the house shake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moving out of Wyoming resulted in large-scale forcing across Southeast Montana. Very warm and moist air resulted in MLCAPE values of 1500-2500 J/KG. In addition, veering winds with height with 35-40kt of deep-layer shear supported an environment capable of producing supercell thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 70 mph was estimated 8 miles ESE of Hammond. Winds downed 6 to 8 inch diameter limbs off a Cottonwood tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere resulted in severe thunderstorm winds over a three day period. Low levels were dry which resulted in predominately dry thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 6-8 diameter tree branches broken along with a power outage and a roof being blown off a dugout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere resulted in severe thunderstorm winds over a three day period. Low levels were dry which resulted in predominately dry thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph along with blowing dust was reported 6 miles NNE of Molt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant spring storm impacted south central Montana May 8-11 as a moist and slow moving storm system impacted the region. Rain began to move into the region late Sunday, May 8th with the heaviest rainfall Monday into Tuesday. Some embedded thunderstorms enhanced rainfall across portions of the region late on Monday, the 9th. Widespread rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches occurred. The heavy rainfall also combined with some mid elevation snowmelt from nearby mountains to enhance flooding in some foothill locations. As a result, widespread lowland flooding was reported along with some damage to roads and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park County officials reported flooding on Billman and Trail Creeks southwest of Livingston due to heavy rainfall and mid elevation mountain snowmelt. Numerous roads flooded (Miller, Trailer Creek, Old Yellowstone, Cokedale, Rendezvous Trail) in the region with water overtopping them with lowland flooding in nearby farmland. Several homes and trailers flooding. Several farms were cutoff and unreachable because of flooded raodways. The Shields River in central Park County ran high and out of it's banks, flooding nearby farmland.

Across northwest Rosebud County, flooding on Home Creek resulted in significant water over Bascom Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant spring storm impacted south central Montana May 8-11 as a moist and slow moving storm system impacted the region. Rain began to move into the region late Sunday, May 8th with the heaviest rainfall Monday into Tuesday. Some embedded thunderstorms enhanced rainfall across portions of the region late on Monday, the 9th. Widespread rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches occurred. The heavy rainfall also combined with some mid elevation snowmelt from nearby mountains to enhance flooding in some foothill locations. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding resulted in road closures occurring across Custer County with numerous roads reported as washed out. Significant flooding was reported along the Powder River as well as its tributaries. Dry, Spring, Strevel and Mizpah Creeks all reported flooding. Major flooding occurred from north of Powderville to Mizpah. Water overtopped Pumpkin Bridge on Pumpkin Creek. A storm spotter 16 miles south of Miles City on Highway 59 indicated this was the worst flooding that they had seen in that area in 25 years. There was significant lowland flooding in the Yellowstone Valley area of Northeast Miles City where Emergency Travel Only was declared for a time. A water rescue was required when a paper carrier in the valley washed off the road. ||Tongue River flooding on May 10-11th resulted in Custer County declaring a flood disaster and a water rescue due to Graveyard Creek washing out Kinsey Road. A levee system was damaged in Miles City. In addition, Custer County ran out of road closed signs due to extent of washed out roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain affected Valley County the hardest, but rainfall over an inch stretched back into central Montana as well. A few locations in the area received three inches of rain. This rainfall brought flash flooding that some areas have never seen, particularly in the Cherry Creek drainage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1430 MST, four to five inches of water flowed over Highway 2 in Glasgow. Most roads in town were flooded. At 1515 MST, three to five foot depths of water was flowing down two coulees on property of an NWS employee four miles northwest of Glasgow. This then resulted in several inches of water flowing over Riverside Drive. At 1730 MST, 10 miles west of Glasgow, the Billingsley Road near Antelope Creek was closed, with water running over the road. At 2020 MST, near Glasgow, water was over the road on Roosevelt Trail between Highway 24 and Lindell Road. At 1630 MST, one mile west of Glasgow, water from flooding in the Cherry Creek drainage was flooding basements. Water was three to four inches up against the affected foundations. At 1647 MST, two miles west of Glasgow, the flooding along Cherry Creek began to flood homes along Skylark Road. The Sunnyside golf course was under water and the flood waters reached U.S. Highway 2. Martin Coulee was flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over Billings, MT at approximately 730pm on Wednesday, June 15th. These thunderstorms progressed east across South Central Montana, basically tracking just north of Interstate 90 from Billings to Hardin. The southern flank of the storm produced persistent winds in excess of 60 mph. ||At 830 pm, the severe winds enhanced over Hardin, MT. National Weather Service officials conducted a survey of the damage and determined that the damage was the result of microburst winds that surged well south of the associated thunderstorm. Local wind speeds were estimated to have reached speeds of 90 mph. The hardest hit area was the southwest side of Hardin. Several businesses experienced at least partial roof damage. This same area saw numerous trees either snapped off or uprooted and homes with shingles blown off them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level circulation over southwest Montana brought widespread heavy rainfall.  This same area received significant rainfall the week before. This rainfall in addition to a heavy snow pack had saturated the ground with streams and rivers running high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cottonwood creek flooded parts of the town of Deer Lodge and down stream.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 1.25 inch diameter hail. The spotter also reported some shingle damage along with dents in a copper roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable southwest flow aloft set the stage for thunderstorm development. Some of these storms turned severe, occurring mainly in Phillips and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm/ranch was significantly damaged by a tornado and damaging downburst winds.  The storm stripped trees bare, four car garages were either destroyed or damaged.  A 1000 gallon fuel tank that was filled that day was blown off it's stand.  The houses received significant damage from hail and the high winds,and the majority of the windows on the two homes were busted out by the wind and hail.  A 2x6 piece of lumber was blown and implanted into the side of one of the houses.  The majority of the damage was downstream with the storm system.  The peak gust was recorded at 86 mph before the sensor was destroyed.  Quarter size hail fell with the storm as well, wiping out nearby field.  The NWS did not receive the tornado report until a month after the event, but radar data verified the possibility of a tornado, and the property owner verified seeing a funnel cloud come from the sky prior to seeking shelter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line developed over portions of South Central Montana just east of Billings and pushed across the southeast portions of the state. Large hail and damaging winds accompanied this squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph was reported. The strong winds broke twigs and snapped 2 inch diameter branches off of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low moved into the four corners region on the 17th and migrated northeast into the Dakotas through the 22nd, bringing another round of heavy precipitation aided by a tap of Gulf of Mexico moisture. Another system passed to the south on the 23rd and 24th, continuing the precipitation across the area. A new all-time daily precipitation record for Billings was set on May 24th. These two systems brought a total of 4 to 7 inches of precipitation to the area through the 25th. From May 29th through May 31st, another Pacific storm system moved northeast through the Billings Forecast Area, producing a half inch to 2 inches of precipitation across the area. A few areas received close to 3 inches of precipitation. The culmination of precipitation events resulted in historic flooding across the Billings Forecast Area. The monthly precipitation for May resulted in the wettest May ever recorded in Billings.||Widespread, severe flooding occurred across the entire Billings Forecast Area. Many roads and bridges were damaged or washed out across the area, including closure of I-90 between Livingston and Springdale eastbound lanes. The Montana Department of Transportation alone estimated over $36 million in damage to state and federal highways across the state of Montana. Hundreds of homes and trailers and multiple businesses experienced flood damage. There was damage to flood control dams. On May 19th, Montana Governor Brian Schweitzer declared an emergency for the state. In addition, the Governor requested that the President declare a major disaster for the State of Montana on June 1st, as a result of substantial widespread flooding. Governor Schweitzer deployed the Montana National Guard in Roundup and Crow Agency to help with flood response efforts May 28th and 29th. Flood emergencies were declared by the Governor for all but three counties in the Billings Forecast area. Emergency declarations included the Crow Reservation in Big Horn County. There were two fatalities, an elderly woman in Boyd in Carbon County fell into a flooded ditch and was swept away, and a man from Huntley in Yellowstone County was killed on an excavator when flooding from Pryor Creek overtook him. Multiple Emergency Operation Centers and Incident Commands were set up across the area, including in Billings in Yellowstone County, and in Park County to coordinate flood relief and response efforts.||A persistant upslope northeast flow into the region resulted in another round of locally heavy rainfall again on May 29-30th. An additional 1-2 inches of rainfall were noted, especially for the Beartooth and Big Horn Mountain foothill areas. This resulted in prolonged or even renewed flooding in some locations through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred on creeks and streams across Carbon County with numerous county roads flooded, closing roads and resulting in significant damage. Emergency Travel only was advised at one point due to the severity of the flooding. Specifically, Rock Creek flooded, resulting in adjacent lowland flooding and closure of Grape Vine Road near Fromberg. Creeks were running out of their banks from the Beartooth Foothills between McLeod and Red Lodge, as well as tributaries of the Clarks Fork of the Yellowstone River from Belfry to Bridger. Five Mile Creek flooded and washed out the bridge on East Pryor Road between Edgar and Pryor. Blue Water Creek east of Bridger flooded and washed out a county road as well. The heavy rainfall also resulted in water running over the spillway at Cooney Dam. This resulted in several homes flooded along Red Lodge Creek. ||On May 25th, significant flooding was reported in the town of Joliet as debris backed up Rock Creek resulting in evacuations. At the peak of this flooding, Highway 212 was closed from Rockvale to Red Lodge as 18 inches of water was reported on Highway 212 through the town of Joliet. Fifty homes flooded in the area and seven people, mainly elderly, had to leave with the assistance of the fire and sheriffs offices. The Joliet Motel reported a foot of water in each of their rooms. ||In addition, an 84-year-old woman drowned after she fell into a flooded ditch near her house. She was going to get her newspaper when she slipped and fell. The woman was swept a short distance downstream from her house near Boyd, where authorities found her body.||Damage estimates based on FEMA reviews and Individual Assistance approvals.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From May 21st and even continuing into the month of June, extensive severe flooding occurred across Big Horn County from the Little Bighorn and Bighorn Rivers, as well as creeks and streams. An Incident Command Center was set up in Crow Agency where the Crow Tribal Chairman declared a disaster. At the height of the flooding, the Big Horn County Sheriffs office is quoted as saying, there is water everywhere.  Hundreds of people were evacuated as flooding affected every community on the Crow Reservation. Fifteen to twenty homes were heavily impacted by the flooding.  Interstate 90 south of Hardin to the Wyoming State line was closed for five days (May 21-26). Water rescues were required on the I-90 frontage road south of Crow Agency. Highway 212 was also closed for a period of time and at one point had water 3 feet deep overtopping the roadway. Railroad tracks and numerous rural roads were washed out in the area. In addition, flooding near Hardin brought down telephone equipment that handles 911 and long-distance calls for major cities and towns in eastern Montana. ||The Bighorn River above Tullock Creek crested at a record 10.86 feet on May 23rd. The Little Bighorn River at Hardin rose above flood stage from May 22nd through May 27th and crested at a record 12.32 feet on May 23rd.||Damage estimates based on FEMA reviews and Individual Assistance approvals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches across the area and mountain snow melt resulted in rapid water runoff and flooding. As a result, the Musselshell River rose above flood stage from Harlowton to Ryegate, as well as in the towns of Roundup and Musselshell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High flows along the Musselshell River from receding floodwaters from May quickly returned to flood as more heavy rainfall moved through the region. Additional rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell across much of Musselshell County during the 6th and 7th of June. This caused rapid water runoff and similar flooding that had just occurred in May. County officials indicated flooding in the town of Roundup. Several roads were washed out and many others closed due to the flooding. Highways 87 and 12 leading into Roundup were inundated and closed. Evacuations of about 30 homes occurred in Roundup. About eight feet of standing water surrounded the Busy Bee Cafe, a local landmark in Roundup. Many houses and businesses in Roundup were under several feet of water. An estimated 50 structures were inundated with water, some up to their rooftops. In addition, four teenagers had to be rescued from the high waters after disobeying orders to stay out of the area and then became stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches across the area and mountain snow melt resulted in rapid water runoff and flooding. As a result, the Musselshell River rose above flood stage from Harlowton to Ryegate, as well as in the towns of Roundup and Musselshell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High flows along the Musselshell River from receding floodwaters from May quickly returned to flood as more heavy rainfall moved through the region. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell across most of Golden Valley County during the 6th and 7th of June. This caused rapid water runoff and more flooding similar to what was experienced in May. County officials indicated flooding in the town of Ryegate and water on Highway 12 near Ryegate. There were reports of several homes being flooded. In addition, a rancher 10 miles southwest of Ryegate received 5 inches of rain which washed out roads and caused flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches across the area and mountain snow melt resulted in rapid water runoff and flooding. As a result, the Musselshell River rose above flood stage from Harlowton to Ryegate, as well as in the towns of Roundup and Musselshell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High flows along the Musselshell River from receding floodwaters from May quickly returned to flood as more heavy rainfall moved through the region. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell across much of Wheatland County during the 6th and 7th of June. This caused rapid water runoff and more flooding similar to what occurred in May. County officials indicated flooding occurred all along the Musselshell River. There was extensive flooding in Harlowton. Highway 12 was inundated and closed leading into Harlowton. The Musselshell River at Harlowton fluctuated above and below flood stage from the 7th through the 14th. The river crested at 8.65 feet on the 9th and 8.54 feet on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches across the area and mountain snow melt resulted in rapid water runoff and flooding. As a result, the Musselshell River rose above flood stage from Harlowton to Ryegate, as well as in the towns of Roundup and Musselshell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall combined with snow melt caused the Tongue River near Birney to fluctuate above and below flood stage throughout the entire month. The river crested at 6.34 feet on the 1st, 7.30 feet on the 12th, 6.94 feet on the 18th and 6.95 feet on the 27th. The 7.30 crest on June 12th was the highest crest on record at the gauge site near Birney. Sandbagging efforts avoided flooding downstream in the St Labre and Ashland areas. Otherwise, mainly lowland ranch and farm land was impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches across the area and mountain snow melt resulted in rapid water runoff and flooding. As a result, the Musselshell River rose above flood stage from Harlowton to Ryegate, as well as in the towns of Roundup and Musselshell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall upstream on the Yellowstone River resulted in the Yellowstone River at Miles City to rise above flood stage. The Yellowstone River at Miles City rose above flood stage June 11th through June 30th and crested at 13.19 feet on the 12th and 14.01 feet on the 28th. The persistent high flows resulted in significant bank erosion along the river. However, no major impacts occurred as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although no significant synoptic spring system moved across the Billings forecast area, warm late June and early July temperatures resulted in rapid mountain snow melt runoff and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported flooding along Rock Creek near 2 Mile Road which resulted in 2 Mile Road north of Red Lodge being closed. In addition, Rock Creek in Red Lodge rose above flood stage on the 29th and continued above flood stage through the end of the month. The river crested at 7.78 feet on the 29th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds moved several round hay bales over 100 yards, part of the way being uphill.  Baseball size hail also fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Considerable forcing and ascent and deep-layer wind fields resulted in widespread and intense thunderstorm activity. Boundary layer dewpoints in the 50s, steep lapse rates and pronounced veering of winds with height fostered an environment favorable for supercells capable of large hail and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 845pm, a tornado briefly touched down at a residence 5 miles east of Molt. A 15 foot grain bin was destroyed, picked up and thrown into an outbuilding 50 feet to the southwest. Additional minor damage occurred to the siding and room on a nearby house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west winds aloft resulted in late night high winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 62 mph was reported at Heart Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west winds aloft resulted in late night high winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 mph was reported at Bynum 13W (the Dellwo MSCN site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low moved into the four corners region on the 17th and migrated northeast into the Dakotas through the 22nd, bringing another round of heavy precipitation aided by a tap of Gulf of Mexico moisture. Another system passed to the south on the 23rd and 24th, continuing the precipitation across the area. A new all-time daily precipitation record for Billings was set on May 24th. These two systems brought a total of 4 to 7 inches of precipitation to the area through the 25th. From May 29th through May 31st, another Pacific storm system moved northeast through the Billings Forecast Area, producing a half inch to 2 inches of precipitation across the area. A few areas received close to 3 inches of precipitation. The culmination of precipitation events resulted in historic flooding across the Billings Forecast Area. The monthly precipitation for May resulted in the wettest May ever recorded in Billings.||Widespread, severe flooding occurred across the entire Billings Forecast Area. Many roads and bridges were damaged or washed out across the area, including closure of I-90 between Livingston and Springdale eastbound lanes. The Montana Department of Transportation alone estimated over $36 million in damage to state and federal highways across the state of Montana. Hundreds of homes and trailers and multiple businesses experienced flood damage. There was damage to flood control dams. On May 19th, Montana Governor Brian Schweitzer declared an emergency for the state. In addition, the Governor requested that the President declare a major disaster for the State of Montana on June 1st, as a result of substantial widespread flooding. Governor Schweitzer deployed the Montana National Guard in Roundup and Crow Agency to help with flood response efforts May 28th and 29th. Flood emergencies were declared by the Governor for all but three counties in the Billings Forecast area. Emergency declarations included the Crow Reservation in Big Horn County. There were two fatalities, an elderly woman in Boyd in Carbon County fell into a flooded ditch and was swept away, and a man from Huntley in Yellowstone County was killed on an excavator when flooding from Pryor Creek overtook him. Multiple Emergency Operation Centers and Incident Commands were set up across the area, including in Billings in Yellowstone County, and in Park County to coordinate flood relief and response efforts.||A persistant upslope northeast flow into the region resulted in another round of locally heavy rainfall again on May 29-30th. An additional 1-2 inches of rainfall were noted, especially for the Beartooth and Big Horn Mountain foothill areas. This resulted in prolonged or even renewed flooding in some locations through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred on creeks and streams across Sweet Grass County resulting in widespread lowland flooding and numerous county roads flooded, closing roads and resulting in significant damage. Numerous bridges also washed out. Specifically, Upper Deer, Lower Deer, Bridger, Sweet Grass, Big Timber and Otter Creeks Flooded. Specific roads flooded include; Anderson, Lower Sweet Grass, Mendinhaul, and Windsor Loop. At the height of the flooding, water was overtopping Interstate 90 from Mile Post 376 to 377. Damage estimates based on FEMA reviews and Individual Assistance approvals.

The Tongue River at Miles City rose above flood stage from May 11th through May 23rd and crested at a record 13.99 feet on May 21st. Damage estimates based on FEMA reviews and Individual Assistance approvals.
